---
title: "Ch 7 — Coordinate Geometry"
subtitle: "Pythagoras between two pairs of numbers, and a midpoint that needs no measurement"
lesson-id: "cbse10.coordinate-geometry"
tier: "cbse10"
ncert-class: 10
ncert-chapter: 7
ncert-chapter-title: "Coordinate Geometry"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: [cbse9.coordinate-geometry, t2.pythagorean-theorem]
unlocks: [cbse11.straight-lines, cbse11.conic-sections, cbse10.trigonometry-intro]
applied-domains: [game-dev, ai-ml, research]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you plot the point $(3, -2)$ on graph paper?
2. Can you state and use the Pythagorean theorem? See
   [`t2.pythagorean-theorem`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd).
3. Do you know what an "x-axis" and "y-axis" are? See
   [`cbse9.coordinate-geometry`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd).
4. Can you simplify $\sqrt{50}$ to $5\sqrt{2}$?

Three yeses, you are ready.
:::

## Touch

Two friends are at points $A = (1, 2)$ and $B = (5, 5)$ on a flat field. Use
the widget — drag $B$ — and watch how the **distance** between them changes.

```{ojs}
viewof bx = Inputs.range([-5, 9], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "B's x"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof by = Inputs.range([-5, 9], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "B's y"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 380, h = 320, padX = 30, padY = 20;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-6, 10]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-6, 10]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const A = [1, 2], B = [bx, by];
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");
  // grid
  for (let i = -6; i <= 10; i++) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(i)).attr("x2", xS(i))
      .attr("y1", yS(-6)).attr("y2", yS(10)).attr("stroke", "#eee");
    svg.append("line").attr("y1", yS(i)).attr("y2", yS(i))
      .attr("x1", xS(-6)).attr("x2", xS(10)).attr("stroke", "#eee");
  }
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(-6)).attr("x2", xS(10))
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(0)).attr("stroke", "#888");
  svg.append("line").attr("y1", yS(-6)).attr("y2", yS(10))
    .attr("x1", xS(0)).attr("x2", xS(0)).attr("stroke", "#888");
  // right triangle
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(A[0])).attr("y1", yS(A[1]))
    .attr("x2", xS(B[0])).attr("y2", yS(A[1])).attr("stroke", "#26a")
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,3");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(B[0])).attr("y1", yS(A[1]))
    .attr("x2", xS(B[0])).attr("y2", yS(B[1])).attr("stroke", "#26a")
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,3");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(A[0])).attr("y1", yS(A[1]))
    .attr("x2", xS(B[0])).attr("y2", yS(B[1])).attr("stroke", "#c33")
    .attr("stroke-width", 2);
  // points
  for (const [P, name] of [[A, "A"], [B, "B"]]) {
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", xS(P[0])).attr("cy", yS(P[1]))
      .attr("r", 4).attr("fill", "#333");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(P[0]) + 6).attr("y", yS(P[1]) - 6)
      .attr("font-size", 12).text(`${name}(${P[0]}, ${P[1]})`);
  }
  const dx = B[0] - A[0], dy = B[1] - A[1];
  const dist = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", h - 6).attr("font-size", 13)
    .text(`Δx = ${dx},  Δy = ${dy},  distance = √(${dx*dx} + ${dy*dy}) = ${dist.toFixed(3)}`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The dashed lines show the horizontal and vertical "legs" of a right
triangle whose hypotenuse is $AB$. The horizontal leg has length $|x_2
- x_1|$, the vertical leg $|y_2 - y_1|$.

## Wonder

1. The widget draws a right triangle every time. Why?
2. If you had a way to compute *the third side* of a right triangle from
   the other two — would that give you the distance?
3. Halfway between $A$ and $B$ — what point is that, exactly? Try a few
   placements of $B$.
4. Could you compute the distance if both points had the same x-coordinate
   (a vertical line)?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(2) Yes — Pythagoras is the whole game. (3) The midpoint coordinates are
the *averages* of the endpoint coordinates. (4) Yes — distance is just
$|y_2 - y_1|$.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Distance via Pythagoras
Find the distance between $A(1, 2)$ and $B(4, 6)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Horizontal leg = $4 - 1 = 3$. Vertical leg = $6 - 2 = 4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$d = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — General formula
Derive a formula for the distance between $P(x_1, y_1)$ and $Q(x_2, y_2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Horizontal leg = $x_2 - x_1$ (or its absolute value). Vertical leg =
$y_2 - y_1$. Apply Pythagoras.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}.$$
</details>

### Problem 3 — Midpoint
Find the midpoint of $A(2, 3)$ and $B(8, 11)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The midpoint splits both legs in half. So both coordinates are averages.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$M = \left(\dfrac{2 + 8}{2}, \dfrac{3 + 11}{2}\right) = (5, 7)$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Section formula (1:2)
A point $P$ divides the segment $AB$ in the ratio $1 : 2$ (so $AP : PB =
1 : 2$). With $A = (3, 4), B = (9, 16)$, find $P$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$P$ is one-third of the way from $A$ to $B$ (because $AP = \frac{1}{3}AB$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$P_x = A_x + \frac{1}{3}(B_x - A_x) = \frac{2A_x + B_x}{3}$? Check: with
ratio $m : n = 1 : 2$, $P = \dfrac{m B + n A}{m + n}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P_x = \dfrac{1 \cdot 9 + 2 \cdot 3}{1 + 2} = \dfrac{9 + 6}{3} = 5$.
$P_y = \dfrac{1 \cdot 16 + 2 \cdot 4}{3} = \dfrac{24}{3} = 8$.
**$P = (5, 8)$.** Verify: $AP = \sqrt{(5-3)^2 + (8-4)^2} = \sqrt{20}$;
$PB = \sqrt{16 + 64} = \sqrt{80} = 2\sqrt{20}$. Ratio = $1 : 2$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 5 — Section formula (general)
For any $A(x_1, y_1), B(x_2, y_2)$ and ratio $m : n$, derive the formula for
the dividing point.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Mimic Problem 4. The dividing point splits each leg of the right triangle
in the same ratio.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$P = \left(\dfrac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n}, \dfrac{m y_2 + n y_1}{m + n}
\right).$$
The midpoint formula is the special case $m = n = 1$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Collinearity check
Check whether $A(1, 1), B(4, 4), C(7, 7)$ are collinear.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
If collinear, $AB + BC = AC$ (one of the three pairs).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = \sqrt{9 + 9} = 3\sqrt{2}$. $BC = 3\sqrt{2}$. $AC = 6\sqrt{2}$.
$AB + BC = 6\sqrt{2} = AC$. **Yes — collinear.**
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Distance formula.** The distance between $P(x_1, y_1)$ and $Q(x_2, y_2)$
is
$$PQ = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}.$$

**Section formula.** The point $P$ that divides the segment $AB$ in the
ratio $m : n$ (with $A = (x_1, y_1), B = (x_2, y_2)$, and $P$ between $A$
and $B$ — *internal* division) is
$$P = \left(\dfrac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n}, \dfrac{m y_2 + n y_1}{m + n}
\right).$$

**Midpoint** is the special case $m = n = 1$:
$$M = \left(\dfrac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \dfrac{y_1 + y_2}{2}\right).$$

:::

A verse:

> *Subtract, then square, then square, then add — under the root they go.*
> *Distance falls out, true and clean — Pythagoras on show.*
> *Cut a segment m to n? Cross-multiply each end —*
> *m times far plus n times near, divide by m plus n.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the coordinates of the point $P$ that divides the segment from
> $A(-1, 7)$ to $B(4, -3)$ in the ratio $2 : 3$.

$m = 2, n = 3$.
$P_x = \dfrac{2(4) + 3(-1)}{5} = \dfrac{8 - 3}{5} = 1.$
$P_y = \dfrac{2(-3) + 3(7)}{5} = \dfrac{-6 + 21}{5} = 3.$

**$P = (1, 3)$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Distance between $(3, 0)$ and $(0, 4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$.
</details>

**W2.** Midpoint of $(-2, 5)$ and $(8, -1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\left(\dfrac{-2 + 8}{2}, \dfrac{5 - 1}{2}\right) = (3, 2)$.
</details>

**W3.** Distance between origin and $(7, -24)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{49 + 576} = \sqrt{625} = 25$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that the points $A(1, 7), B(4, 2), C(-1, -1), D(-4, 4)$ are
the vertices of a square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute all four sides and the two diagonals.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = \sqrt{9 + 25} = \sqrt{34}$.
$BC = \sqrt{25 + 9} = \sqrt{34}$.
$CD = \sqrt{9 + 25} = \sqrt{34}$.
$DA = \sqrt{25 + 9} = \sqrt{34}$. All sides equal.
Diagonals: $AC = \sqrt{4 + 64} = \sqrt{68}$. $BD = \sqrt{64 + 4} = \sqrt{68}$.
Equal. So it's a rhombus with equal diagonals — a **square**. ✓
</details>

**P2.** Find $k$ so that $(k, 2)$ is equidistant from $(3, 4)$ and $(-3, 7)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$d_1^2 = d_2^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(k - 3)^2 + (2 - 4)^2 = (k + 3)^2 + (2 - 7)^2$
$\Rightarrow k^2 - 6k + 9 + 4 = k^2 + 6k + 9 + 25$
$\Rightarrow -12k = 21 \Rightarrow k = -\dfrac{7}{4}$.
</details>

**P3.** Find the ratio in which the point $(-3, p)$ divides the segment
from $A(-5, -4)$ to $B(-2, 3)$. Hence find $p$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use section formula on the x-coordinate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let ratio $m : n$. $-3 = \dfrac{-2m - 5n}{m + n} \Rightarrow -3m - 3n =
-2m - 5n \Rightarrow m = 2n$. Ratio $2 : 1$.
$p = \dfrac{2(3) + 1(-4)}{3} = \dfrac{2}{3}$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Find the area of the triangle with vertices $A(2, 3), B(-1, 0),
C(2, -4)$ using the formula
$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}|x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Plug in carefully. Watch signs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\frac{1}{2}|2(0 - (-4)) + (-1)((-4) - 3) + 2(3 - 0)|$
$= \frac{1}{2}|2 \cdot 4 + (-1)(-7) + 2 \cdot 3|$
$= \frac{1}{2}|8 + 7 + 6| = \frac{21}{2}$ sq units.

(Note: the area-of-triangle formula was retained in the rationalised
syllabus despite some early confusion. It's a standard 3-marker.)
</details>

**S2.** Find the centroid of the triangle with vertices $A(1, 4), B(-2, -1),
C(7, 3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The centroid is at the average of the three vertex coordinates.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$G = \left(\dfrac{1 - 2 + 7}{3}, \dfrac{4 - 1 + 3}{3}\right) = (2, 2)$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**Q1 (1 mark — MCQ).** Distance between $(0, 0)$ and $(a \cos\theta,
a \sin\theta)$ is:
(a) $a$ (b) $a^2$ (c) $\sqrt{a}$ (d) None.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{a^2 \cos^2\theta + a^2 \sin^2\theta} = \sqrt{a^2(\cos^2\theta +
\sin^2\theta)} = \sqrt{a^2} = |a|$. **(a) $a$** (assuming $a > 0$).
</details>

**Q2 (1 mark — fill-in).** The midpoint of the segment joining $(-3, 5)$
and $(7, -1)$ is ____.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\left(\dfrac{-3 + 7}{2}, \dfrac{5 - 1}{2}\right) = (2, 2)$.
</details>

**Q3 (2 marks).** Find the value of $y$ for which the distance between the
points $P(2, -3)$ and $Q(10, y)$ is $10$ units.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sqrt{64 + (y + 3)^2} = 10$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(y + 3)^2 = 36 \Rightarrow y + 3 = \pm 6 \Rightarrow y = 3$ or
$y = -9$.
</details>

**Q4 (2 marks).** If the point $P(x, y)$ is equidistant from $A(5, 1)$ and
$B(-1, 5)$, prove that $3x = 2y$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$PA = PB \Rightarrow PA^2 = PB^2$. Expand.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x - 5)^2 + (y - 1)^2 = (x + 1)^2 + (y - 5)^2$.
Expand: $x^2 - 10x + 25 + y^2 - 2y + 1 = x^2 + 2x + 1 + y^2 - 10y + 25$.
Simplify: $-10x - 2y = 2x - 10y \Rightarrow 8y = 12x \Rightarrow 3x = 2y$.
$\square$
</details>

**Q5 (3 marks).** Find the ratio in which the line segment joining
$A(-2, -3)$ and $B(3, 7)$ is divided by the y-axis. Find the y-coordinate
of the dividing point.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The y-axis has $x = 0$. Use the section formula's x-coordinate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0 = \dfrac{3m - 2n}{m + n} \Rightarrow 3m = 2n \Rightarrow m : n = 2 : 3$.
$y = \dfrac{2 \cdot 7 + 3 \cdot (-3)}{5} = \dfrac{14 - 9}{5} = 1$.
**Ratio $2 : 3$, dividing point $(0, 1)$.**
</details>

**Q6 (3 marks).** Show that the points $A(7, 10), B(-2, 5), C(3, -4)$ are
the vertices of an isosceles right triangle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute the three sides. Then check Pythagoras.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$AB = \sqrt{81 + 25} = \sqrt{106}$.
$BC = \sqrt{25 + 81} = \sqrt{106}$.
$AC = \sqrt{16 + 196} = \sqrt{212}$.
$AB = BC$ — isosceles. $AB^2 + BC^2 = 106 + 106 = 212 = AC^2$ — right
angle at $B$. **Isosceles right triangle.** $\square$
</details>

**Q7 (5 marks — long answer).** The line segment joining $A(2, -2)$ and
$B(3, 7)$ is divided by a point $P$ such that $\dfrac{AP}{PB} = \dfrac{2}
{3}$. Find: (a) the coordinates of $P$; (b) whether $P$ lies on the line
$2x + y - 4 = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Section formula with $m : n = 2 : 3$. Then plug into the line equation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a)** $P_x = \dfrac{2(3) + 3(2)}{5} = \dfrac{12}{5}$,
$P_y = \dfrac{2(7) + 3(-2)}{5} = \dfrac{8}{5}$.
$P = \left(\dfrac{12}{5}, \dfrac{8}{5}\right)$.

**(b)** Check: $2 \cdot \dfrac{12}{5} + \dfrac{8}{5} - 4 = \dfrac{24}{5} +
\dfrac{8}{5} - \dfrac{20}{5} = \dfrac{12}{5} \ne 0$.
So **$P$ does not lie on the line**.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar 7.3 Q1–Q12 are direct
distance/midpoint applications like Q3–Q4; Exemplar 7.4 Q1–Q12 are
section-formula problems like Q5–Q7.

**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q26 mirrors Q5 (section
formula on an axis). CBSE 2023 Standard, Q34 mirrors Q7.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$d = (x_2 - x_1) + (y_2 - y_1)$."** No — that gives a *signed* sum,
   not a length. Square, add, then root. (Even more dangerous: forgetting
   to square — losing the negative-sign safety.)
2. **"Section formula: $\dfrac{n x_2 + m x_1}{m + n}$ vs $\dfrac{m x_2 +
   n x_1}{m + n}$."** It's $\dfrac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n}$ — *the
   coefficients cross over*. Memorise: with ratio $m : n$, the point
   leans towards $B$ when $m$ is larger.
3. **"The y-axis has $y = 0$."** No — the **y-axis** has $x = 0$ (every
   point on it has zero x-coordinate). The **x-axis** has $y = 0$.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you derive both the distance and section formulas from
   Pythagoras and similar triangles, without notes?
2. Did Q5 and Q7 fall out cleanly with at most one hint?
3. Can you spot when a problem is asking for "ratio in which X divides
   Y" and translate it to a section-formula equation?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Q4 and Q7.
- **0–1 yes:** practise the *Discover* problems again. Coordinate
  geometry is mostly bookkeeping — once you trust the formulas it
  becomes mechanical.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.coordinate-geometry`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd) —
  axes, coordinates, plotting points.
- [`t2.pythagorean-theorem`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd) —
  the engine of the distance formula.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse11.straight-lines`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/09-straight-lines/index.qmd) —
  slope, equation forms.
- [`cbse11.conic-sections`](../../tier-cbse-11-class-11/10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd) —
  circles, ellipses, parabolas, hyperbolas as loci.
- [`cbse10.trigonometry-intro`](../08a-trigonometry-intro/index.qmd) —
  the unit-circle viewpoint uses these formulas.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 10, Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 7.1–7.2: distance and section formula. (The
  area-of-a-triangle topic, Section 7.3, was retained in the
  rationalised text and remains examinable.)
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t2.coordinate-plane`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd) —
  coordinate plane from scratch.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Every collision check ("did the bullet hit the
target?") is a distance comparison in 2D or 3D. Every "spawn the enemy
halfway between two waypoints" is a midpoint computation.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI/ML.** k-nearest-neighbour, k-means clustering, and almost every
geometric ML algorithm rest on the distance formula generalised to $n$
dimensions. Same Pythagoras, more terms under the root.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Coordinate geometry — René Descartes, 1637 — was the
breakthrough that made geometry algebraic. Almost every modern
mathematical structure (manifolds, vector spaces, fibre bundles) is a
descendant of this idea: *put coordinates on geometry*.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** 3Blue1Brown (the *Touch* widget builds the visual
  Pythagoras intuition); Russian school (problem-first); Bhāskara
  (compact *Name* + verse).
- **Source notes:** Q4 and Q7 follow standard CBSE 2020/2023 patterns.
  The area formula in S1 is the determinant form widely tested.
:::
